title: Quiz: Qubit Modalities
description: Test your understanding of different qubit technologies, their physics, and comparative advantages

questions.question: Which qubit modality typically achieves the longest coherence times (T2)?
questions.explanation: Trapped ion hyperfine qubits achieve the longest coherence times, with T2 values ranging from seconds to over 50 seconds in state-of-the-art systems. This is because hyperfine transitions are first-order insensitive to magnetic field fluctuations, and ions are well-isolated from their environment in ultra-high vacuum.

questions.question: What is the primary mechanism enabling two-qubit gates in neutral atom quantum computers?
questions.explanation: Neutral atom quantum computers use the Rydberg blockade mechanism for two-qubit gates. When atoms are excited to high-lying Rydberg states (n~50-100), they exhibit strong van der Waals interactions (V ~ C6/R^6) that prevent simultaneous excitation of nearby atoms, enabling controlled entanglement.

questions.question: The 'quality factor' for quantum gates is defined as T2/t_gate. Which statement about this metric is correct?

options.text: Superconducting qubits have the highest quality factor due to fast gates
Trapped ions have high quality factors despite slower gates due to long coherence
Photonic qubits have infinite quality factor since photons don't decohere
The quality factor is the same for all modalities when properly optimized

questions.explanation: Trapped ions achieve very high quality factors (T2/t_gate ~ 10^5 to 10^7) because their coherence times (seconds) vastly exceed their gate times (microseconds). While superconducting qubits have faster gates (~10-100 ns), their shorter coherence times (~100 us) result in lower quality factors (~10^3 to 10^4).

questions.question: What is the main challenge for photonic quantum computing that makes it different from other modalities?

options.text: Photons require cryogenic cooling to maintain coherence
Single-qubit gates are difficult to implement with high fidelity
Two-qubit gates are probabilistic due to weak photon-photon interactions
Photon generation rates are fundamentally limited to one per second

questions.explanation: The main challenge for photonic quantum computing is that photons don't naturally interact with each other, making deterministic two-qubit gates extremely difficult. The KLM protocol achieves universal computation but with probabilistic gates (success rate ~1/16 for CZ). Modern fusion-based approaches improve this to 50-75% success rates but still require significant overhead.

questions.question: Which of the following are advantages of superconducting qubits? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Qubits are identical since they are natural atoms
Flexible circuit design for customized connectivity

questions.explanation: Superconducting qubits offer: (a) fast gates at nanosecond timescales, (b) compatibility with semiconductor fabrication processes, and (d) flexible circuit design for engineered connectivity. They do NOT use natural atoms (option c applies to trapped ions and neutral atoms), and they require dilution refrigerators at ~15 mK, not room temperature (option e).

questions.question: The DiVincenzo criteria specify requirements for quantum computation. Which of the following is NOT one of these criteria?

options.text: Ability to initialize qubits to a known state
Coherence times at least 1000x longer than gate times

questions.explanation: The DiVincenzo criteria require coherence times 'long compared to gate times' but do not specify a numerical threshold like 1000x. The actual criteria are: (1) scalable system, (2) initialization, (3) long coherence relative to gates, (4) universal gate set, and (5) measurement capability. The 1000x figure is a common rule of thumb but not part of the formal criteria.

questions.question: In trapped ion systems, what physical mechanism enables the Molmer-Sorensen two-qubit gate?

options.text: Direct Coulomb interaction between ion electronic states

questions.explanation: The Molmer-Sorensen gate couples ions through their shared motional modes (phonons) in the trap. By applying appropriately detuned laser beams, the internal qubit states become entangled via the collective motion of the ion chain, without actually exciting the motional modes at the end of the gate operation.

questions.question: Neutral atom quantum computers can achieve reconfigurable qubit connectivity by physically moving atoms using optical tweezers, while superconducting circuits have fixed connectivity determined at fabrication.
questions.explanation: True. This is a key advantage of neutral atom systems - atoms can be moved in real-time using optical tweezer arrays, allowing dynamic reconfiguration of the qubit connectivity graph. Superconducting circuits have connectivity fixed by the physical layout of the couplers fabricated on the chip.
